questions.question: What are the five types of boundaries described in the lesson?

options.text: Personal, professional, social, mental, and spiritual
Time, task, emotional, digital, and physical
Work, home, relationship, financial, and health
Hard, soft, flexible, rigid, and conditional

questions.explanation: The lesson describes five types of boundaries: time boundaries (fixed work start/end times), task boundaries (saying no to excess requests), emotional boundaries (not absorbing others' emotions), digital boundaries (turning off notifications), and physical boundaries (defined workspace, actual breaks).

questions.question: What is the basic formula for setting a boundary?

options.text: Explain in detail why you cannot do something, then apologize repeatedly
Name the limit and offer what you can do, if appropriate
Say no firmly and walk away without explanation
Ask someone else to communicate the boundary for you

questions.explanation: The basic boundary formula is: name the limit + offer what you can do (if appropriate). For example: 'My plate is full right now. I can do X by Friday, or Y by tomorrow — what is the priority?' You do not need to justify your limits with a detailed reason.

questions.question: What does the lesson say about the guilt that comes with setting boundaries?

options.text: Guilt means you have set the wrong boundary and should reverse it
You should never feel guilty about boundaries if they are truly needed
Guilt is normal, is a feeling not proof you are doing something wrong, and usually decreases with practice
Guilt only happens when you set boundaries with people who do not respect you

questions.explanation: Guilt is the most common barrier to boundaries. The lesson explains that guilt is a feeling, not proof that you are doing something wrong. It usually decreases after you have maintained the boundary for a few days. You can feel guilty AND maintain the boundary — discomfort is not proof of wrongdoing.

questions.question: Why does the lesson recommend starting with 'micro-experiments' when building boundaries?

options.text: Because large boundaries always fail
Because small wins build confidence, and each successful boundary makes the next one easier
Because managers only accept very small changes
Because micro-experiments do not cause any pushback

questions.explanation: You do not need to overhaul your entire life. Starting with one small boundary experiment (like setting a specific email cutoff time) lets you see results, manage guilt, and build confidence. Small wins build confidence, and each successful boundary makes the next one easier.

questions.question: When are individual boundaries NOT enough, according to the lesson?

options.text: When you feel any resistance to setting them
When the problem is systemic — chronic understaffing, toxic culture, or workloads that are genuinely impossible
When your coworkers do not set boundaries either
Boundaries are always enough if you set them consistently

questions.explanation: Sometimes individual boundaries cannot fix systemic problems like chronic understaffing, a culture that punishes setting limits, a manager who consistently disrespects boundaries, or workloads that are genuinely impossible. In these cases, you may also need systemic advocacy, documentation, or professional support.
